query: folic acid and congenital heart defects in Down syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Intracardiac Shunts -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of Intracardiac shunt formation is multifactorial and combines genetic and external factors. Genetic factors include chromosomal deletions, trisomies, and single-gene mutations. For example, 40 to 50% of those born with trisomy 21 or Down syndrome have ASD, VSD, TOF, or TGA. [2] Maternal factors that increase the risk for intracardiac shunt formation are pregestational diabetes, marijuana use, rubella, ibuprofen, influenza, febrile illness, and vitamin A exposure. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of Down syndrome. Identify atrial septal defects as the most common cardiac abnormalities in patients with Down syndrome. Summarize the use of ultrasound, amniocentesis, and chorionic villus sampling in the prenatal diagnosis of Down syndrome. Outline the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care and improve outcomes for patients affected by Down syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Deterrence
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal care plays a critical role in managing HLHS. Routine ultrasounds, including fetal echocardiography, facilitate early detection, enabling advanced planning and specialized perinatal care. Pregnant individuals should also avoid teratogens such as alcohol, tobacco, and specific medications like angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors. Ensuring optimal maternal health through proper management of conditions like diabetes or hypertension and maintaining adequate folic acid intake during pregnancy may further reduce congenital anomalies. Advances in research and genetic screening may eventually lead to preventive strategies or targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Congenital Cardiac Defects (CHD)
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital cardiac defects are by far the most common and leading cause associated with morbidity and mortality in patients with Down syndrome, especially in the first 2 years of life. Though different suggestions have been made about the geographical as well as seasonal variation in the occurrence of different types of congenital cardiac defects in trisomy 21, so far none of the results have been conclusive. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac referral should arranged for all the patients regardless of the clinical signs of congenital heart disease. If present, this should be corrected within the first 6 months of life to ensure optimum growth and development of the child.

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Congenital Cardiac Defects (CHD)
retrieved_docs.content: The other cardiac defects associated with trisomy 21 are secundum atrial defect (10%), tetralogy of Fallot (6%), and isolated PDA (4%), while about 30% of the patients have more than one cardiac defect. There is geographical variation in the prevalence of the cardiac defect in Down syndrome, with VSD being the most common in Asia and secundum type ASD in Latin America. The reason behind this difference in the prevalence of different types of CHD in different regions is still unclear, and many factors such as regional proximity have been found to contribute. [7] Because of such a high prevalence of CHD in patients with Down syndrome, it has been recommended that all patients get an echocardiogram within the first few weeks of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome -- Etiology -- Environmental and Maternal Factors
retrieved_docs.content: While specific environmental exposures have not been definitively linked to HLHS, maternal factors such as pregestational diabetes, advanced maternal age, and certain teratogenic exposures may modestly increase the risk. Nutritional deficiencies (ie, low folate levels) and maternal obesity have also been associated with congenital heart defects, though their direct role in HLHS remains speculative.

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Congenital Cardiac Defects (CHD)
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of CHD in babies born with Down syndrome is up to 50%. The most common cardiac defect associated with Down syndrome is an atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD), and this defect makes up to 40% of the congenital cardiac defects in Down syndrome. [7] It is said to be associated with the mutation of the non-Hsa21 CRELD1 gene [7] [12] The second most common cardiac defect in Down syndrome is a ventricular septal defect (VSD), which is seen in about 32% of the patients with Down syndrome. Together with AVSD, these account for more than 50% of congenital cardiac defects in patients with Down syndrome. [7] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: With the recent advances in the medical practice, development of surgical techniques for the correction of congenital disabilities, and improvement in general care, there has been a tremendous increase in the survival of infants and life expectancy of patients with Down syndrome. A Birmingham (United Kingdom) study done almost 60 years ago showed that 45% of infants survived the first year of life, and only 40% would be alive at 5 years. [55] A later study conducted about 50 years after that showed that 78% of patients with Down syndrome plus a congenital heart defect survived for 1 year, while the number went up to 96% in patients without the anomalies. [56] This rise in the life expectancy of these patients should continue to rise significantly because of the developments in medical science. Healthcare facilities aim to provide proper and timely management to these patients and to help them to have a fulfilled and productive life. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The remaining 20% to 25% of patients with TOF have an associated syndrome or a chromosomal abnormality, with the most frequent being trisomy 21 (ie, Down syndrome) and the 22q11.2 deletion syndromes, which range from the more severe DiGeorge syndrome that is accompanied by dysmorphic facies, palatal abnormalities, immune deficiencies, hypocalcemia, and learning disabilities, to the less severe Sprintzen or velocardiofacial syndrome which does not have the immune deficiency and hypocalcemia. Because 22% to 48% of patients with a 22q11.2 microdeletion have an interrupted aortic arch, and 24% have a right-sided aortic arch, TOF is not infrequently associated with aortic arch malformations. When a patient with TOF presents with pulmonary valve atresia, the association with 22q11.2 microdeletion increases to 40%. Thus, genetic testing is currently recommended in fetuses with TOF to assess for the 22q11.2 microdeletion, especially since outcomes are worse than in those who do not carry the microdeletion. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrial Septal Defect -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: ASDs occur as singular defects but are associated with Mendelian inheritance, aneuploidy, transcription errors, mutations, and maternal exposures. ASDs are noted in patients with syndromes such as Down, Treacher-Collins, thrombocytopenia-absent radii, Turner, and Noonan. These conditions occur as a result of Mendelian inheritance. Maternal exposure to rubella and drugs like cocaine and alcohol may also predispose the unborn fetus to developing an ASD.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- Cardiovascular System
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital heart disease (CHD) is one of the most common congenital defects, with most (approximately 80%) occurring due to multifactorial causes arising through various combinations of genetic and environmental issues and about 20% attributed to chromosomal abnormalities or teratogens. Down syndrome and velocardiofacial syndrome are the two most commonly seen syndromes in patients with congenital heart disease. [13] There are two main categories of CHDs; cyanotic and acyanotic. Cyanotic - right-to-left shunts Tetralogy of Fallot Transposition of the great vessels Tricuspid atresia Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR) Truncus arteriosus Acyanotic - further classified into: Left-to-right shunts Atrial septal defect (ASD) Ventricular septal defect (VSD) Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) Outflow obstruction Pulmonary stenosis Aortic stenosis Coarctation of the aorta

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Folic acid (vitamin B9) is a water-soluble vitamin used to manage and treat megaloblastic anemia. Folic acid has FDA approval for treating megaloblastic and macrocytic anemias due to folic deficiency. This activity describes the indications, mechanism of action, and contraindications for folic acid as a valuable agent in managing megaloblastic anemia and preventing other disorders. One of the beneficial roles of folate appears to be its ability to reduce homocysteine levels in neural tube defects. In addition, this activity will highlight the adverse event profile and other key factors (eg, off-label uses, dosing, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent to healthcare teams dealing with conditions that can benefit from supplemental folic acid.

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Canal Defects -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Further down, the complete AV canal defect may further be classified based on the AV valve morphology (Rastelli classification). The Rastelli classification is based on the insertion of the chordae and the morphology of the superior bridging leaflet. [11] Type A: This is the most common type associated with Down syndrome. The superior bridging leaflets by means of chordal attachments are inserted onto the left ventricle. Type B: This is the least common. The superior bridging leaflets by means of chordal insertion are extended onto the body of the right ventricle. Type C: This is associated with the tetralogy of Fallot (TOF), transposition of great arteries (TGA), etc. [12] There are free-floating superior bridging leaflets with no chordal insertions.

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Septal Defect -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: AVSD accounts for approximately 4% to 5% of all congenital heart defects and is strongly associated with chromosomal abnormalities, particularly trisomy 21 (Down syndrome). The condition is a leading cause of early heart failure in infants. However, long-term outcomes after surgical repair have significantly improved due to advances in diagnostic techniques, surgical strategies, and perioperative care. This course provides a comprehensive overview of AVSD, including its embryology, epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical presentation, diagnostic evaluation, management strategies, complications, and overall clinical significance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات) is clinically associated with cardiac conduction disorders, suggesting that patients with this type of anemia may be predisposed to abnormalities in the heart’s electrical signaling. Postoperative complete heart block represents a specific conduction disorder that is formally mapped within cardiac electrophysiology classifications. Recognizing the relationship between folate‑deficiency anemia and conduction disturbances, together with accurately categorizing postoperative complete heart block, is important for proper diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1283
retrieved_docs.content: Antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to treat or prevent anemia, and both folic acid and vitamin B12 are specific types of these preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2253
retrieved_docs.content: Folate deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات) is clinically associated with conduction disorders, indicating that patients with this type of anemia may exhibit abnormalities in the heart’s electrical conduction system. Postoperative complete heart block is mapped to conduction disorders, reflecting that this specific postoperative arrhythmia is classified as a severe form of conduction system impairment. Additionally, conduction disorders co‑occur with bradycardia (انخفاض معدل ضربات القلب), meaning that slowed heart rates are frequently observed alongside various conduction abnormalities. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected nature of electrolyte‑related anemia, postoperative cardiac conduction blocks, and low heart‑rate presentations in the assessment and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2846
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier is applied to the prevention and control of mitral valve stenosis, threatened abortion, and fetal distress syndrome. Preventive and control strategies for mitral valve stenosis aim to limit valve narrowing and reduce cardiac complications. In cases of threatened abortion, early identification and appropriate interventions are essential to prevent progression to miscarriage and to protect maternal and fetal health. For fetal distress syndrome, vigilant monitoring and timely therapeutic measures are crucial to manage fetal compromise and improve outcomes for both mother and child. Integrating prevention and control measures for these conditions supports better patient care and reduces the risk of adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 5 -- Community 2920
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacotherapy is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for a range of conditions, including premature birth (ولادة قبل الأوان), mortality (التموت), extra‑hepatic biliary obstruction (انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد), mitral valve stenosis (تضيُّق الصمام المترالي), and bilateral lower‑extremity ulcers (قرحات الطرفين السفليين). In each of these clinical scenarios, the use of medication‑based treatment is considered permissible and can play a role in managing the underlying pathology or associated symptoms, underscoring the versatility of pharmacologic interventions across diverse medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: The oral tablet labeled “folic acid 1 MG / vitamin B12 0.5 MG” contains the two active ingredients vitamin B12 and folic acid, providing a combined supplement of these essential nutrients.
